coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of eco-friendly hydrogel strips for the simultaneous quantification of heavy metal ions in aqueous environment
coredata.dc:creator: Mohan, Anandhu
Prakash, John
coredata.dc:description: A biocompatible optosensor that can be used for detection of toxic heavy metal ions such as, Cd2+ and Hg2+ through naked eye is presented here. The sensor consists of biodegradable PEGDA (Poly(ethylene glycol) diacrylate) hydrogel incorporated with plant origin fluorescent pigments as main components. Curcumin and anthocyanin, naturally occurring fluorophores are obtained from turmeric and red cabbage respectively in a facile and green route. The transparent-swelling attuned hydrogel containing optimized quantities of plant pigments yield white emission (Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage, CIE: 0.307, 0.342) on blue excitation. The white emitting optosensor makes visible colour changes to heavy metal ions (HMIs) viz., Hg2+ (green) and Cd2+ (blue). In addition, the adoption of a multivariate method, Multivariate Curve Resolution – Alternating Least Square (MCR-ALS), enhances the possibility of concurrent and precise quantification of both HMI in fresh water at ppb level. We have shown that the designed optosensor has very good analytical fidelity, as root mean square error prediction is less than 0.03. The ease of fabrication (<30 min), availability of ingredients, cost effective nature (₹ 30 or $ 0.4), possibility of adaptive dimension of gel (strip or button), biodegrading nature of hydrogel, compatibility to fiber optic spectrometer, etc. Make the optosensor a cost effective and also an eco-friendly analytical tool.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Heavy metal ion
Naked eye sensor
Plant pigments
White light emission
MCR-ALS
Eco-friendly
Hydrogel

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of White Light Emitting (WLE) mixture
Interaction of meatal ions towards plant pigments and WLE mixture
Spectroscopic identification & photophysical characterization of plant pigment extracts
Optimization of source wavelength & composition of fluorophores to get white light emission
Interaction of HMI with white light emission

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The electronic absorption spectra (a), fluorescence emission spectra (b, excitation at 424 nm) of turmeric extract obtained in ethanol solvent and same for cabbage extract (d and e –excitation at 370 nm) obtained in 1% HCl –methanol solutions are displayed. The chemical structure of photophysically characterised (c) curcumin molecule from turmeric and (f) cyanidin-3-diglucoside-5-glucoside molecule from red cabbage extracts. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Quenching of fluorescence emission intensity of extracted plant pigments: (a) curcumin (10 μM) and (b) anthocyanin (60 μM) in presence of 10 μM solutions of Cd2+ and Hg2+ ions.

Selection of excitation LED wavelength: (a) Electronic absorption spectra of curcumin and anthocyanin extract are plotted in the same scale and the excitation wavelength is optimized as 370 nm with OD < 0.32. (b) Fluorescence spectra (Ex – 370 nm) of white light emitting mixture (Black), Curcumin (Green), and Anthocyanin (Magenta) in white light emitting mixture. (c) Chromaticity plot for CIE coordinates of white light emission (0.324, 0.339) in acidic aqueous solution. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Average swelling ratio (bar diagram) and optical transparency (red line-symbol diagram) of PEGDA hydrogels at concentrations from 30% to 60% w/v. (b) Photos of flexibility and the transparency of the optimized PEGDA hydrogel. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Images showing sensing of HMIs using white light emitting opto-sensor (b & e) Fluorescence Spectra of sensor at various concentrations (20–50 μM) of Cd2+ & Hg2+ ions. (c) Movement of CIE locus (towards bluish green color). (d) Linear plot of CCT against concentration of Cd2+ ions (R2 > 0.985) (f) Movement of CIE locus (towards green color). (g) Linear plot of CCT against concentration of Hg2+ ions (R2 > 0.996). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence spectra of sensors at various concentrations (0–50 μM) of both Hg2+ and Cd2+ ions. (b) Resolved fluorescence spectra and (c) concentration profile from resolved data obtained using MCR-ALS.
Photo-physical characterization of plant origin fluorophores in 1% HCl-TDW medium.
A comparative view of detection limits of existing sensors with newly developed PEGDA- WLE optosensor.

row.entry: Optical sensors based on aptamer detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Root mean square error in prediction (RMSEP) of the quantity of HMI in the river water samples.

row.entry: Root mean square error in prediction (RMSEP)

head.ce:title: Fabrication of eco-friendly hydrogel strips for the simultaneous quantification of heavy metal ions in aqueous environment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Basic and Applied Sciences, Central University of Tamil Nadu, Thiruvarur, 610 005, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Basic and Applied Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Basic and Applied Sciences, Central University of Tamil Nadu, Thiruvarur, 610 005, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A biocompatible optosensor that can be used for detection of toxic heavy metal ions such as, Cd2+ and Hg2+ through naked eye is presented here. The sensor consists of biodegradable PEGDA (Poly(ethylene glycol) diacrylate) hydrogel incorporated with plant origin fluorescent pigments as main components. Curcumin and anthocyanin, naturally occurring fluorophores are obtained from turmeric and red cabbage respectively in a facile and green route. The transparent-swelling attuned hydrogel containing optimized quantities of plant pigments yield white emission (Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage, CIE: 0.307, 0.342) on blue excitation. The white emitting optosensor makes visible colour changes to heavy metal ions (HMIs) viz., Hg2+ (green) and Cd2+ (blue). In addition, the adoption of a multivariate method, Multivariate Curve Resolution – Alternating Least Square (MCR-ALS), enhances the possibility of concurrent and precise quantification of both HMI in fresh water at ppb level. We have shown that the designed optosensor has very good analytical fidelity, as root mean square error prediction is less than 0.03. The ease of fabrication (<30 min), availability of ingredients, cost effective nature (₹ 30 or $ 0.4), possibility of adaptive dimension of gel (strip or button), biodegrading nature of hydrogel, compatibility to fiber optic spectrometer, etc. Make the optosensor a cost effective and also an eco-friendly analytical tool.

ce:section.ce:para: Fresh water is one of the major concerns of the living organisms particularly to the survival of human society. Of the 0.008% of the total available freshwater on earth, only 0.5% is accessible to the living organisms including ∼8 billion human beings. The accessibility to fresh water is dwindling day by day mainly due to human activities resulting in water pollution. The World Health Organisation has published a list of pollutants that affect the drinking water quality. Pollutants are categorized mainly into microbial pollutants (all disease-causing organisms such as Algae, Fungus, Virus, Bacteria, Protozoa etc.) and chemical pollutants (any chemicals, including heavy metal ions) which is pernicious to living organisms. Though microbial contamination affects the health faster than chemical contamination [1], long-term exposure to chemical pollutants causes severe health problems. The non-degradable and persistent nature of HMI makes them a major pollutant of great concern. The advancement of various industries, mining units, agriculture sector etc. imparts an increased release of heavy metals to surface water and even groundwater resources. The accumulation of HMI in living organisms can cause serious health issues such as damage to kidneys, liver, reproductive system, central nervous system and DNA damage [2]. Among the top four big pollution related ailments recorded, the adverse effects of HMI, especially cadmium (Cd) and mercury (Hg) tops the list. The Itai-itai disease of 1912 caused by Cd poisoning; Minamata (1956) and Niigata-Minamata disease (1965) caused by Hg poisoning [3]. The Cd and Hg are considered hazardous among heavy metals, because of their tendency to accumulate in liver and kidneys and they are hard to metabolize. As per WHO, the maximum permissible values of cadmium and mercury in drinking water are 6 and 3 μg/L (6 and 3 ppb) respectively [1]. The long half-lives (Cd: ∼35 years and Hg: ∼10 years) [4] of both heavy metals in the living body can make them ‘silent killers’. So, the early detection of such HMI in fresh water is very essential.

Traditionally, techniques such as colourimetry, spectrophotometry, electrochemical, chromatographic, etc. and technical advancements like atomic absorption spectroscopy and spectrometry are in practice for detection and quantification of HMI [5] [–] [8], while some of the techniques lack the selectivity and sensitivity, most of the methods requires expertise-mandate protocols. Among the techniques that are highly sensitive and selective, require sophisticated and expensive facility & instrumentation.

The efficiency of fluorescence spectroscopy in sensitive and selective monitoring, possibility of multi-parameter analysis, prospect of layman friendly devising, etc. Make it an attractive option towards the early detection of HMI. If an HMI is responding to any of the spectral observables, viz., intensity, emission maxima, quantum yield, lifetime, etc., of a suitable fluorescent probe the sensing can be performed even without meddling with the complexity of the analyte [9] [–] [16]. Very familiar fluorophores used for the detection of HMI are either organic molecules; mostly dyes, quantum dots (QDs) or carbon dots (CDs) [17] [–] [28]. Nowadays QDs and CDs have acquired more interest over dyes because of colour tuning properties and photo-stability. Nevertheless, the need of multi-step synthetic procedure, difficulty in controlling the spectral parameters while catering to industrial demands and necessity of expensive methodologies for the routine characterization demand well trained experts as analyst. Importantly, most of the organic dyes and quantum dots are environmentally hostile due to its toxicity and chemical-photo reactivity. The overall cost of making the above-mentioned sensor is very high and developing a user-friendly sensor is very difficult.

The focus of this work is to make white light emitting sensor that selectively changes its emission in contact with HMI. Natural fluorophore molecules of plant origin possess high molar absorption coefficient and decent fluorescence quantum yields. Earlier scientists have developed white emission from plant origin pigments, such as chlorophyll, anthocyanins and curcumins [29,30], which are non-toxic, environment friendly and biodegradable. A judicious selection of plant origin fluorophore pigments, which are selective to HMI and yield white emission can be a remedy for the situation. Immobilization of suitable natural origin pigment to a sensor platform, preferably a compatible hydrogel, can yield a suitable user friendly, low cost, bio-compatible and reusable opto-sensor for the HMI.

Plant pigments were extracted from turmeric (Curcuma longa), and red – cabbage (Brassica oleracea) purchased from a local shop in Thiruvarur, Tamil Nadu, India. Analytical grade hydrochloric acid, methanol and ethanol procured from Sisco Research Laboratories were used as extracting media. The metal ions, chlorides of mercury, cadmium, iron, copper, cerium, sodium, calcium, magnesium and manganese were purchased from SD Fine Ltd. Hydrogel, poly (ethylene glycol) diacrylate (PEGDA, Mn = 700) and photo-initiator, 2-hydroxy-2-methyl-propiophenone were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich & co. Triple distilled water (TDW) was also used as a solvent.

The electronic absorption measurements were carried out in JASCO UV-VIS-NIR V670 Spectrophotometer. Fluorescence emission spectra of samples were recorded using PERKIN ELMER LS55 & AGILENT CARY ECLIPSE fluorescence spectrometers. Osram Sylvania color calculator software was used for plotting CIE diagrams.

Root rhizome of the turmeric plant was crushed using mortar and pestle. Then curcumin was extracted using ethanol as solvent. This solution was centrifuged at 5000 rpm. A clear yellow solution was obtained with a major dye component as curcumin, which was filtered, dried and used for the study [29,30]. The leaf of red-cabbage was boiled in distilled water. This broth was centrifuged around 5000 rpm, and a clear dark red solution was collected. This solution was further diluted using water, which contains cyanidin-3-(sinapoyl)-diglucoside-5-glucoside as a major anthocyanin component [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of White Light Emitting (WLE) mixture
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of the white light emitting (WLE) mixture was optimized to get white light emission at single wavelength excitation as follows. Firstly, an excitation wavelength was chosen as 370 nm, such that the wavelength can excite both the plant pigments simultaneously. The composition of the plant extract components was optimized by employing basic mathematical calculation on fluorescence intensity of each component (Table ESI, S-2). Fluorescence emission spectra of the concentration-optimized mixture in 1% HCl - TDW was then measured and CIE coordinates were calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of meatal ions towards plant pigments and WLE mixture
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction of electropositive metal ion with the WLE mixture was performed as follows. Same concentrations of different metal ions (Mn2+, Ce2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Na+, Hg2+ and Cd2+) having same counter ion, Chloride ion (Cl−) is added to the WLE mixture and the fluorescence intensity change was noted. The selection of same counter anion chloride (Cl−) negate the influence of counter ions.

Required concentration of PEGDA was mixed with 2% w/v of 2-hydroxy-2-methyl-propiophenone (photo-initiator) and TDW. The mixture was homogenized and the solution was poured into a glass mold of dimension 30 mm (L) x 8 mm (W) x 1 mm (T) and polymerized under UV radiation of 365 nm for 5 min [32] in Hally Instruments - ultraviolet viewing cabinet. The thickness of the hydrogel formed was confirmed using a screw-gauge (Eisco: Micrometer Screw Gauge – Digital SKU PH0087).

WLE mixture is mixed with optimized concentration of PEGDA hydrogel and photo-initiator. The precursor is shaken well to form a uniform solution. Afterwards, the precursor was poured into a glass mold and polymerized under UV radiation of 365 nm for 5 min.

The fidelity of the optosensor in presence of possible interferences such as humus substances, particulate matter, etc. are studied using water samples collected from Vettar River, Thiruvarur –Tamil Nadu. Samples were collected in Borosilicate glass containers and were spiked with a different constant amount of Hg2+ and Cd2+ ions (10–50 μM). It is then filtered through disposable MILLEX filters (33 mm, and 0.22 μm). The filtered river water is clear and having a low amount of humic substance as 112 ± 5 ppm [33]. The emission spectra from the optosensor are recorded after dipping it in 30 s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic identification & photophysical characterization of plant pigment extracts
ce:section.ce:para: Turmeric Extract: The electronic absorption spectrum of turmeric extract shows a characteristic broad absorption from 300 to 500 nm with a peak around 424 nm (ethanol), corresponding to the S0 to S1 transition (Fig. 1 a). The extract gives a green emission with a maximum at 534 nm (ethanol) on 424 nm excitation, which is a characteristic green emission of curcumin molecule [34] (Fig. 1b & c). Presence of curcumin molecule is also confirmed by analysing the 1H -NMR spectral features of the turmeric extract (Fig. S1, supplementary data).

Red cabbage extract: Electronic absorption and emission spectra of cabbage extract is recorded in 1% HCl-Methanol mixture to identify the major fluorophore components. The electronic absorption spectrum is resolved using a non-linear Gaussian curve resolution method due to the presence of more than one component. A strong absorption at 283 and the absorption maxima at 530 nm (1% HCl-Methanol) is recognised as anthocyanin peak of cyanidin-3-diglucoside- 5-glucoside and sinapoyl group attached to cyanidin-3-diglucoside-5-glucoside shows a peak at 315 nm [35] (Fig. 1d). i.e., cyanidin-3-diglucoside- 5-glucoside gives two absorption maxima in acidic medium, first one resembles to the gallic acid (283 nm) absorption due to the structural similarity in the polyphenol part of anthocyanin. The fluorescence emission maxima at 450 nm is the emission of polyphenol part of cyanidin-3-(sinapoyl)-diglucoside-5-glucoside. However, in acidic medium an emission peak at 580 nm corresponds to characteristic emission of anthocyanin i.e. cyanidin-3-diglucoside-5- glucoside [36] (Fig. 1e & f).

The amount (concentration) of natural pigments extracted varies with extraction procedure and the source. Therefore, the concentration of the extracted fluorophores is calculated and expressed in terms of molar absorption coefficient. The molar absorption coefficient ‘ε’ of curcumin (λabs - 420 nm) and anthocyanin (λabs - 528 nm) are respectively 55000 mol−1dm3cm−1 in ethanol and 16466 mol−1dm3cm−1 in 1% HCl-methanol mixture [29,30,37]. These known values are used to estimate the approximate concentration of the fluorophore molecules in the extract.

The extracted fluorophores are characterized using electronic absorbance and fluorescence emission measurements in 1% HCl-TDW medium (Fig. S2, supplementary data). The approximate fluorescence lifetime of the fluorophores are also calculated from their absorption data [38] (Fig. S3, Table S1, supplementary data). The fluorescence lifetime of polyphenols, anthocyanin and curcumin are found to be 0.19, 6.18 and 0.26 ns respectively. The photo-physical parameters of plant origin pigments are tabulated in Table 1 .

The well-known selective interaction of HMI; Cd2+ and Hg2+ studied with fluorophoric extract. Mercuric ion shows quenched emission in the turmeric extract. This is because of the disruption of π electron delocalization in curcumin, a major component in the extract, when Hg2+ ions coordinate the β-diketone functional group (Plausible mechanism: Fig. S4 in supplementary data). In addition to fluorescence emission study, conductometric and potentiometric studies reveal that there is no specific interaction between curcumin with cadmium ions [39] (Fig. 2 a). The fluorescence emission of cabbage extract shows substantial enhancement on interaction with cadmium ions. The enhancement is due to the formation of a 1:2 complex of metal ion with the polyphenolic moiety of the major fluorescent component in the extract, i.e. cyanidin-3-diglucoside-5-glucoside. A single cadmium ion can bind with two polyphenolic groups of neighboring anthocyanin [40] and hence increases the structure rigidity around the polyphenolic group, which leads to the enhancement of fluorescence in polyphenolic emission. The emission from the other part is unaffected and it can be seen in the spectra (Fig. 2b). The anthocyananin fluorescence remains unaffected in the presence of mercuric ions, due to the bulk size of ions. Thus, there exists no interaction between anthocyanin and mercuric ions. The plausible mechanism is shown in Figure S4c & d of supplementary data. Both binding mechanisms of HMI towards fluorophores are confirmed using Job Plot experiment (S4.a & b, supplementary data) Since HMIs show selective interaction towards extract, a sensor can be developed using these fluorophores.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of source wavelength & composition of fluorophores to get white light emission
ce:section.ce:para: A single wavelength LED excitation as the light source will avoid interferences from stray light, minimizes scattering and ease the devising. The excitation wavelength was chosen as 370 nm (blue light) because of enough overlap with the absorption spectra of both fluorophoric extracts (Fig. 3 a). In addition, the total optical density of the mixture is maintained below 0.32, A < 0.32, to avoid primary and secondary inner filter effects.

The anthocyanin is weakly fluorescent in nature compared with curcumin and there exists a significant overlap of absorption and emission spectra of polyphenols, anthocyanin and curcumin. So, the concentration ratio of the two fluorescent extracts is optimized theoretically as 1:6.2 (Calculation is given in supplementary data). For the experimental simplicity in view of sample preparation, the ratio was chosen as 1: 5 (8.6 μM curcumin and 43.5 μM anthocyanin). The composition yields white light emission on blue excitation (370 nm) and the CIE coordinates are extremely close to the pure white coordinate (0.324, 0.339) (Fig. 3b & c) [41].
The spectral additive nature of emission with minimal loss on the red side yields a broad white light emission, covering a complete visible region, i.e., from 400 to 700 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of HMI with white light emission
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the WLE mixture towards Hg2+ and Cd2+ metal ions is measured in presence of various other HMI: Fe2+, Cu2+, Ce3+ and Mn2+ions with Cl− as a counter anion. The interaction study using the fluorescence emission reveals, HMIs other than Hg2+ and Cd2+ do not make any impact on the White Light Emission. To ease the visualisation, a plot of colour temperature against the HMI is also displayed (Fig. S5, supplementary data). HMIs other than cadmium and mercury, the color temperature (CT) shows a constant value near 5536 K, same as that of the white light emission. An enhancement in color temperature can be observed with cadmium and mercury resulting from a change in emission color. Therefore, it is clear that WLE mixture shows a very good selectivity towards mercuric and cadmium ions.

The possibility of other inferences such as H+ ion concentration (pH) as well as common metal ions is also studied. Anthocyanins are well known for their sensitivity towards acidic pH. In order to assure that the pH variation is not interfering with the HMI sensing, the changes caused by a wide range of acidic pH (6–1, practically less probable in real life) on WLE is studied using fluorescence emission and CIE coordinates. The trace of CIE locus shows an entirely different pattern which can be easily distinguished from changes caused by heavy metal ions (Fig. S6, supplementary data). Also, the emission can be easily calibrated with respect to particular pH to eliminate the slight interference, if exist. Similarly, the influence of common metal ions (CMIs) was examined. It is found that similar to pH, interaction of CMIs shows different patterns for the emission spectra, CIE coordinates, and locus (Figure S7.b, supplementary data). The emission color changes to blue due to the quenching of anthocyanin and curcumin emission, which resulted in quenching of green and red color.

Popularly known biocompatible and biodegradable hydrogel PEGDA is used as a physical support for the sensor. The chemical composition is optimized by monitoring optical transparency and swelling ratio [42]. The optical transparency increases with increase in concentration, PEGDA hydrogel is opaque in nature at lower concentrations. The optical transparency increases to a maximum value of 83% at 55% w/v and then lowers (Fig. 4 a). The swelling ratio is also monitored as a trend of decrease in the swelling ratio from 30% w/v to 55% w/v. After 55% w/v, the swelling ratio increases rapidly. In addition, the gels of higher concentration are harder than that of gels with lower concentration of PEGDA and they brittle on slight elevation of applied stress. The concentration of hydrogel with flexibility, maximum optical transparency and minimum swelling ratio was optimized as 55% w/v and was used for further studies. Also, the shelf life of hydrogels was determined as 36 weeks in dry condition as well as in hydrated form.

The prepared opto-sensor yields white light emission with the CIE coordinates of (0.307, 0.342) (Fig. S8, supplementary data), which is closer to pure white emission. The production cost of a 30x8x1 mm3 sensor is about ₹ 30 per sensor. The cost can be again reduced to about ₹ 8, if the shape of sensor is changed to a button of diameter 10 mm and thickness 1 mm. An optimal time for the effective response towards HMIs is obtained as 5 min (Figure S8.d, supplementary data). The fluorescence emission responses reach a stable value within 5 min and in further studies, the responses are collected at 5 min. Also, the emission intensity remains the same even after 1 month (36 days). In addition, the sensor shows stable white light emission with the same CT value of 5536 K. These observations suggest that the fluorophores stay inside the hydrogel matrix for a much longer time compared to the response time.

In presence of HMIs the opto-sensor shows significant color change on blue excitation (370 nm). This is measured by immersing sensors in individual solutions containing 10 μM of HMIs for 5 min and the emission color is monitored under UV light. The Cd2+ ions change emission color from white to bluish green, whereas mercuric ions change it to green. These changes can be observed in Fig. 5 .a.

A series of fluorescence measurements is carried out using the sensor in presence of various concentrations of Hg2+ and Cd2+ ions. In the presence of cadmium, an enhancement in the blue-green region (420–550 nm) is observed. This enhancement is caused by the complex formation of a polyphenolic group of anthocyanin with cadmium ions, which leads to the change of emission colour from white to bluish green. The complex formation of mercuric ions with curcumin molecules shows a relative enhancement in the green region and as a result, the emission colour changes from white to green region. The mercuric ions quenches the fluorescence emission of curcumin but the anthocyanin component remains intact as a result an overall enhancement in the green region is observed. The change in color can be visualised using the direction of CIE coordinates locus (Fig. 5c & f). The linear plot of color temperature against concentration of HMIs shows good linearity with R2 > 0.985 and 0.996 respectively. The average LOD and LOQ values of the sensor obtained from the triplicate measurements are 0.37 and 1.25 μM.

The performance of the PEGDA based optosensor is compared with various existing optical-electrochemical sensors and are tabulated in Table 2. It is clear from Table 2 that the optosensor designed is a highly sensitive and it has comparable sensitivity with other advanced optical and electrochemical sensors. In addition, our PEDGA WLE optosensor has the special feature of simultaneous quantification of Cd2+ and Hg2+ without changing any of its configuration or instrumental parameters.

Though the white light emitting optosensor could see (even naked eye) the HMI, simultaneous visualisation of both ions and ions in presence of interfering media like CMI or extreme pH variation is difficult.

The fluorescence emission spectra on blue LED excitation show characteristic signature of enhancement on lower wavelength region and quenching on higher wavelength region. Analytical precision in the simultaneous measurement is made feasible using Multivariate Curve Resolution – Alternating Least Square (MCR-ALS). Adopting few mathematical constraints like non-negativity and unimodality, the MCR-ALS algorithm precisely quantifies the amount of HMI in the river water. MCR-ALS resolves both the components with minimal residuals along with composition [51].

The scope of this methodology is significant due to the availability of a library of sensor molecules/nanomaterials with known spectral features unique to particular environments, HMI, or biologically important scenarios. The simplicity of design, availability of pigment molecules, ease of fabrication, portability of equipment, etc. are the beneficial features of this optosensor. The proof-of-concept work envisages the possibility of real-time monitoring and simultaneous quantification of non-fluorescent species in low scattering samples.

An optosensor is designed by incorporating white light emitting mixture into PEGDA hydrogel for the naked eye detection of HMI. The white light emitting mixture is obtained from plant pigments – anthocyanin and curcumin – and their concentration as well as ratio is optimized to avoid inner filter effects. An LED excitation using a blue LED of 370 nm, the sensor yields a white light emission of CIE coordinates (0.307,0.342). The white light mixture is incorporated into physically optimize (swelling ratio – 10% & transparency – 83%) PEGDA hydrogel yields the Optosensor. Since cadmium and mercuric ions are having accumulative properties, detection must be done for concentrations lower than the permissible values (6 and 3 μg/L for Cadmium and Mercury). In the presence of HMIs, the sensor changes its emission color (Hg2+ - green & Cd2+ - bluish green) and is clearly visible in naked eye under the UV chamber. The sensor has a response time of 5 min and LOD of ∼0.2 μM. This sensor is used for the simultaneous and selective detection of HMI i.e. mercuric and cadmium ions in real samples using MCR-ALS algorithm. The possibility of interfering factors pH and common metal ions are studied and have been easily distinguished. Also, the optosensor is non-toxic to the environment, biocompatible, yields reproducible results and a low-cost tool (∼₹ 30 or $ 0.4).
The authors thank UGC Start up grant No.F.30443/2018(BSR) for the funding.

Anandhu Mohan: Collected the data, Contributed data or analysis tools, Performed the analysis, Wrote the paper. John Prakash: Conceived and designed the Formal analysis, Contributed data or analysis tools, Performed the analysis, Wrote the paper.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2021.110045.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heavy metal toxicity and the environment

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Tchounwou PB, Yedjou CG, Patlolla AK, Sutton DJ. Heavy metal toxicity and the environment. EXS 2012;101:133-164. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-7643-8340-4_6.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The evolution of official lessons: the Japanese experience of the'Big Four'Pollution diseases through the lens of international aid

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gomez OA. The Evolution of Official Lessons: The Japanese Experience of the’Big Four’Pollution Diseases Through the Lens of International Aid. J Altern Perspect Soc Sci 2008;1:81-100.
Kozlowski H, Kolkowska P, Watly J, Krzywoszynska K, Potocki S. General Aspects of Metal Toxicity. Curr Med Chem 2014;21:3721-3740. https://doi.org/10.2174/0929867321666140716093838.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Helaluddin ABM, Khalid RS, Alaama M, Abbas SA. Main analytical techniques used for elemental analysis in various matrices. Trop J Pharm Res 2016;15:427-434. https://doi.org/10.4314/tjpr.v15i2.29.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on various electrochemical techniques for heavy metal ions detection with different sensing platforms

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bansod BK, Kumar T, Thakur R, Rana S, Singh I. A review on various electrochemical techniques for heavy metal ions detection with different sensing platforms. Biosens Bioelectron 2017;94:443-455. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2017.03.031.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent trends in macro-, micro-, and nanomaterial-based tools and strategies for heavy-metal detection

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrochemical analysis by atomic absorption and emission
